title: Chapter Nine: Hazel's Mirror
content: The humming woke me at three in the morning.

Not from the attic this time. Not the thin, reedy sound I'd grown used to hearing between floorboards on certain nights when the fog pressed closest. This humming came from the walls themselves — a low, resonant vibration that I felt in my sternum before I heard it with my ears. Like someone singing with their lips pressed flat against the plaster.
I lay in the dark, breath held, and tracked it. It moved. Not wandering the way the attic sounds wandered — lost and searching. This sound knew where it was going. It led somewhere.

I got out of bed. The cold hit me immediately, the kind of coastal cold that doesn't settle on your skin so much as pass through it. I was wearing Julian's cardigan again. I didn't remember putting it on. The fog outside was so thick that the window had become a flat grey square, no depth to it, as if the world ended at the glass.
The humming led me to the kitchen.

I stood in the doorway, bare feet on tile, and listened. The stove. The old iron stove that I'd never once used because Julian preferred the smaller gas ring in the studio, because the kitchen was my space and therefore not a space that mattered. The humming came from behind it.

I pulled the stove away from the wall. It scraped across the tile with a sound like a complaint, and the humming grew louder. Not louder the way a radio turns up — louder the way a voice becomes clearer when someone stops whispering. As if it had been waiting for me to listen.
One brick behind the stove sat slightly proud of the others. I touched it and pulled my hand back.
It was warm.

Not lukewarm. Not the residual heat of a chimney shared with the next room. Warm the way a handprint stays warm on something you've just let go of. In the January dark of that cottage, with my breath making clouds, that brick radiated heat like a living thing.
I pried it loose.

Behind it: a journal. Leather cover cracked and soft, the color of old blood. It had been there a long time. I pulled it out and the smell hit me — linseed oil and something sharper underneath. Turpentine, maybe. And defiance. I know that's not a smell. But I smelled it anyway.
The first page read, in a hand both elegant and furious: *Property of Hazel Buena, 1889.*
I carried it to the kitchen table and turned on the one lamp that worked — the one with the green glass shade that made everything look like it was underwater. And I read.
Hazel Buena was an artist.

Not a woman who painted, which is what the town of Blackwood Vale would have called her. An artist. The kind whose hand moved before her mind could catch up, whose sketches captured something true and a little dangerous about their subjects. The journal was full of them — pressed between pages, yellowed at the edges but still vivid. A fisherman's wife with one eye on the sea. A child holding a bird with a broken wing, her expression not pitying but fierce. A self-portrait that made me stop breathing: Hazel at twenty-three, hair unbound, paint on her jaw, staring out of the page like she dared me to look away.
Every sketch was signed *E. Whitmore*.
Her husband's name.
I turned pages faster. The story assembled itself the way a fracture line assembles under pressure — you don't see it until the thing breaks, and then you can't see anything else.

Edmund Whitmore. A painter of mediocre talent and enormous ambition. He had married Hazel in 1884 and, within a year, had begun exhibiting work that was clearly, unmistakably hers. The compositions. The brushwork. The particular quality of light she rendered — luminous, almost liquid, as if the sun in her paintings was wet. Critics praised him. Collectors paid. And Hazel, who had made every stroke, signed his name instead of her own because that was the arrangement. Because he told her it was the arrangement.

When she objected — and the journal made clear that she did, repeatedly, with increasing desperation — Edmund changed tactics. He didn't argue. He didn't defend. He performed concern. He told the town that Hazel was *delicate*. That she suffered from *nerves*. That she was *not in her right mind* and he was bearing the burden of care with Christian patience. He said this at the general store. At the post office. At the very gallery that sold her work under his signature. And the town, which had watched Hazel walk through the market with paint still under her fingernails and fire in her eyes, nodded along and said, *Poor thing. Good man, Edmund.*

The same architecture. Different materials. Different century, different medium — but the same house built on the same foundation. A man claiming a woman's work as his own. A man defining a woman's mind as broken when she refused to be defined by him.

Julian paints me as he sees me. Which means as he wants me. The soft edge of the jaw. The downcast eyes. The aurelia — that strange bioluminescent glow that follows me — edited into something decorative. A light he can use to see himself. Not a light I made.

Hazel's sketches were stunning. Even a century and a half later, even on yellowed paper, even signed with a thief's name — they were stunning. And they were not hers. Not legally. Not publicly. Every image she had brought into the world wore another person's name like a mask pressed so tight it became the face beneath.
I closed the journal. My hands were shaking.

Then I felt it — the familiar gathering in my sternum, the sensation I'd come to associate with a wound unlocking. But this one was different. It didn't crack open. It *doubled*. Like two keys turning in the same lock at the same time.
The fourth wound.
I understood it not as a single injury but as a pair — two hands held mirror-image across a dividing line. And I understood what they were for.
*Illusion Weaving*. The ability to make things appear. To make things disappear. To show what was hidden. To hide what needed protecting.
And *Identity Recovery*. The ability to restore a stolen sense of self.
Two sides of one coin. One to cover. One to uncover.

I should not have done what I did next. I should have read the rest of the journal first. I should have waited for daylight. I should have done any number of sensibly postponed things. But the wound was open and the magic was pulsing through me and I wanted — I *needed* — to know.

I held my hands over the kitchen table, palms down, and spoke the words that arrived in my mouth like they'd been waiting there all my life. Not Latin, not any language I recognized. Something older and simpler, the kind of words you say before you learn to lie.
*Show me who I am without the curse.*
The room folded.
Not collapsed — folded. Like a piece of paper creasing along a line I hadn't seen. The kitchen table became a pane of glass, cold under my fingers, and on the other side of it I saw myself.
Two of me.

One stood on the road that led to Julian's lighthouse. The other stood on the path that led into the forest, toward the cabin where Leo Hartwell kept his books and his quiet and his steady, undemanding presence. Both Aurelias were real. I could feel them both — the ache in my chest split cleanly into two distinct frequencies, two directions of want.

The Aurelia on the lighthouse road was walking. Not striding, not strolling — walking the way you walk when something is pulling you. Her shoulders were curved forward. Her feet moved without consulting her. She looked like a woman in a current, and the current was Julian. The gold of his attention, the warmth of his certainty, the way he said *mine* like it was the most natural word in the mouth. She was moving toward him the way metal moves toward a magnet — not because she chose the destination, but because the field left no other direction.

The Aurelia on the forest path was walking, too. But her gait was different. Her shoulders were back. Her chin was up. She wasn't being pulled; she was *going*. Each step was a decision, placed deliberately on the dirt the way you set down a word you mean. The forest was dark ahead of her — but she wasn't afraid of the dark. She was walking into it because she wanted to know what was there. Because the not-knowing had become worse than any risk.

I pressed my palms flat against the cold glass. I could feel the chill of it sinking into my bones. Both Aurelias looked at me through the divide, and both of them were my face, and both of them were true.
The one walking toward Julian walked like she was being pulled.
The one walking toward the forest walked like she was choosing.
And the difference between being pulled and choosing was the only difference that mattered.

It was the thesis statement of everything. Of the curse, of the wounds, of every Buena woman who had stood where I was standing and felt the undertow of someone else's certainty dragging her out to sea. The curse didn't remove your choices. It made you forget you had them. It filled your field of vision so completely that *choosing* seemed like a word from a language you'd once known but couldn't quite translate.
I had always had a choice. The fog — Julian's fog, the curse's fog — had merely convinced me otherwise.

I pressed harder against the glass. I wanted to reach through. I wanted to grab the Aurelia on the forest path and pull her close and ask her how it felt to walk like that, like every step belonged to her. But the glass held. Cold and absolute.
Then it shattered.

Both Aurelias collapsed into me at once — a collision of selves so violent that I fell backward off the chair and hit the floor. My skull cracked against tile. My chest heaved. I was not one person or two; I was one person again who had seen herself doubled, and the reintegration hurt like a wound closing too fast, skin puckering around a foreign body that hadn't been removed.

I lay on the kitchen floor and shook. The lamp had fallen over but hadn't broken. Light pooled across the tile, green and wavering, and in that light I saw that the journal had fallen open to a page I hadn't read.
The handwriting was different here. Smaller. Shaking. The controlled fury of the earlier entries had worn down to something rawer.
*Your identity is the one thing they can't actually steal. They can cover it. They can mirror it. But they can't take it. You have to give it away.*
*Don't.*

I don't know how long I stayed on that floor. Long enough for the shaking to subside. Long enough for the kitchen to stop folding and reassemble itself into something solid and cold and ordinary. The stove was still pulled away from the wall. The warm brick sat beside the gap like a missing tooth.

When I finally sat up, my hands found something without my asking them to. The wooden comb. Leo's comb. I had taken it from the studio — I must have, though I didn't remember doing it. It was simply there in my palm, the way your hand finds a railing in the dark.
I ran it through my hair.

The wood grain moved across my scalp in slow, deliberate strokes. I felt each tooth of the comb like a point of contact between my body and something honest. No softening. No arrangement. Just the clean drag of wood through tangled hair, and the quiet rasp of it, and my own skull beneath my own hand.
I looked at the window.
The fog thinned.

Not all the way. Not the dramatic clearing of a spell broken — nothing so theatrical. It just... thinned. For one unambiguous second, the grey wall outside my kitchen dissolved enough to show what lay beyond it.

The forest path. Dark and rutted and real, cutting through the black pines like a seam through dark cloth. And beyond it, faintly — so faintly I might have imagined it but I did not imagine it — the Boundary Stones. Glowing. A low, steady pulse of amber light, the way a signal fire burns when it has been lit for a very long time and refuses to go out.
The world existed outside Julian.
The fog rolled back in, thick and total, erasing everything. The window became a flat grey square again. The cottage closed around me like a fist.
But I had seen it.

I set the comb on the table beside Hazel's journal. I touched the warm brick one more time. Then I sat in the green light and understood the shape of what the curse had taken from me, and what it had only borrowed.

It had borrowed my direction. It had covered my choices with fog and called the fog the world. But it had not taken my identity. That was the lie inside the architecture — the load-bearing lie, the one that held the whole structure up.

I had to give it away. And I had been giving it away. Every time I walked toward the lighthouse because walking felt easier than choosing. Every time I let Julian's version of me stand in for the real one. Every time I mistook the pull for the path.
Hazel was right. They can cover it. They can mirror it. But they can't take it.

I sat in that kitchen until dawn, and I did not move toward the lighthouse, and I did not move toward the forest. I sat still and let both directions exist inside me equally — the pull and the choice — and I did not give either one permission to decide for me.
That was the beginning.
